question: Can we distinguish actionable prescriptions from observational findings in ASPIRATIONAL lessons to reduce false-positive prescription gap metric?
hypothesis: Adding actionable classifier to enforcement_router.py will reduce false-positive ASPIRATIONAL count by ~40-50%
expect: actionable ASPIRATIONAL ~120-150 of 244 total; orient.py shows filtered actionable gap
method: classify_actionability(rule_text): imperative verbs at sentence start, must-modal, Fix: prefix, backtick code command, colon-imperative pattern

results.orient_py_shows: 33% actionable gap (was: 72% raw, was: >50% threshold never triggered correctly for min-sharpe=9)

actual: CONFIRMED: 121/244 actionable (49.6%) — within expected 120-150 range. 123/244 (50%) observational findings. Prior 84% ASPIRATIONAL rate inflated by 2x. orient.py now shows 33% actionable gap (Sharpe≥9 filtered) with top gap = L-533 (genuine command not observation).
diff: Expected 120-150 actionable: got 121 (CONFIRMED). Reduction factor 0.496 (not the ~0.40-0.50 predicted). Colon-pattern check added false positives for Falsified-if sentences but effect on rate marginal. orient.py threshold changed 0.5→0.15 to show actionable gap (was never triggering for Sharpe≥9 filtered set).
